title: Working with parts that emerged specifically in adulthood
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The parts we built to survive adulthood 

 Most of the parts work I've encountered focuses on childhood. The wounded inner child. The exile from when you...
htmlContent:
The parts we built to survive adulthood

Most of the parts work I've encountered focuses on childhood. The wounded inner child. The exile from when you were five. The protector that showed up when you were eight. And that's real. That's necessary work. But here's the thing: not all of our parts formed when we were small.

Some of them showed up later. In our twenties. Our thirties. Even our forties and fifties. Parts that emerged specifically in adulthood, shaped by experiences that our fully grown nervous system never learned to handle.

I've been sitting with this idea for months now. Because when I first started learning about Internal Family Systems and parts work, I kept waiting to find the little version of myself that held a specific wound. And sometimes I found her. But other times, I found a part that felt very much like an adult. A part that didn't have a child's voice or a child's logic. A part that spoke in full sentences and carried adult-level shame, adult-level rage, adult-level despair.

These parts confuse us. We expect parts to be younger versions of ourselves. So when a part shows up that feels like it formed at twenty-eight, we question whether it's "really" a part at all. We think maybe we're just being dramatic. Maybe this is just who we are now.

But no. These are parts too. And they need our attention just as much as the younger ones do.

Let me tell you about one of mine. There's a part that showed up in my late twenties, after a series of betrayals in close relationships. This part is hypervigilant about loyalty. It scans every interaction for signs that someone is about to abandon me. It's not a child part. It doesn't feel young. It feels like a weary adult who has learned that people leave. That part emerged in adulthood because that's when the specific wound happened.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, talks about how parts can form at any age. The system doesn't stop creating parts just because we've grown up. Our brain keeps trying to protect us, keeps trying to manage overwhelming experiences, keeps trying to organize around pain. And it uses whatever resources it has at the time.

An adult brain has more resources than a child's brain. More language. More cognitive capacity. More life experience. So the parts that form in adulthood often look different. They might be more articulate. They might have elaborate belief systems attached to them. They might feel less like feelings and more like philosophies about how the world works.

But they're still parts. They're still not the whole of who you are. And they're still carrying something that needs to be witnessed.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work specifically addresses how parts form across the lifespan, not just in childhood.

Why adult-formed parts feel different

There's a reason these parts can be harder to identify. They're better at hiding. Think about it.

A child part might show up as a sudden drop in your mood. A feeling of smallness. A wordless sense of terror. It's obvious that something younger has taken the wheel. But an adult part? It shows up as your carefully reasoned opinion. Your firmly held belief. Your "this is just how I see the world" stance.

It doesn't feel like a part because it feels like you. Your adult self. Your real self. Your considered perspective.

But here's the distinction that changed everything for me: a part is anything that has a job. Anything that carries a specific responsibility in your internal system. Anything that has a fixed role it plays, over and over, regardless of whether that role is actually helpful in the current moment.

So when you have a belief that says "I can't trust anyone because everyone eventually leaves," and that belief shows up automatically every time someone gets close to you, regardless of their actual behavior? That's a part. Even if you're forty. Even if the belief feels like wisdom earned through experience.

It's wisdom. But it's also a part. Both things can be true.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how adult trauma survivors develop coping mechanisms that are specific to their developmental stage. These aren't the same as childhood adaptations. They're more sophisticated. More integrated into adult identity. And harder to separate from the self.

I worked with a client once who had a part that formed in her early thirties, after a prolonged period of workplace bullying. This part was a relentless self-critic, but not in the way we usually talk about inner critics. It didn't call her stupid or worthless. It told her she wasn't trying hard enough. It told her she needed to be more strategic, more careful, more perfect. It sounded reasonable. It sounded like good advice from a concerned mentor. But it was running her life, and it was exhausting her.

That part wasn't a child. It was a protector that had emerged in adulthood to help her survive a specific adult threat. And it had never been told that the threat was over. That she had left that job years ago. That she was safe now.

Wild, right? How parts can keep working long after their job is done.

The specific challenges of working with adult-formed parts

There are three challenges I keep encountering when working with these parts. I want to name them directly, because naming things helps us see them.

First, these parts often resist being identified as parts. They don't want to be seen as separate from you. They believe they are you. And they have good reasons for that belief. They formed when you were already a coherent adult, so they integrated into your sense of self more completely than childhood parts did. They feel like your identity, not like a part of your identity.

Second, these parts often have more power in the system. They're more sophisticated, more articulate, more convincing. They can argue you out of your own curiosity. They can talk you into staying identified with them. They have better logic, better evidence, better reasons for why they're right and why you should keep listening to them.

Third, these parts are often connected to legitimate adult experiences. You really were betrayed. You really did lose that job. You really did experience that loss. The part's beliefs aren't based on misinterpretation the way a child's might be. They're based on actual events that happened to your adult self. So questioning the part can feel like gaslighting yourself.

But here's what I've learned: you don't have to question the reality of what happened. You only have to question whether the part's response is still necessary, right now, in this moment, with the resources you have available today.

That's a gentler approach. It doesn't say "you're wrong about what happened." It says "thank you for protecting me through that. I'm not in that situation anymore. Can we try something different now?"

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. For adult-formed parts that developed in response to boundary violations, this book can offer language and structure that helps the part feel heard and eventually release its grip.

How to approach these parts with curiosity instead of force

So how do we actually work with these parts? How do we approach something that feels like the center of our adult self and say "I see you as a part"?

Slowly. That's the honest answer. Slowly and with immense respect.

I don't try to bypass these parts or override them. I don't tell them they're wrong. I don't try to "heal" them by proving that their beliefs are inaccurate. Because they're not inaccurate. They're just incomplete. They're based on real experiences, but they don't account for the full range of what's possible now.

Here's what I do instead. I start by acknowledging the part's legitimacy. I say something like "I see that you've been working really hard to keep me safe. Thank you. I want to understand what you're protecting me from, and I want to understand what you need me to know."

Then I listen. Not with the goal of changing the part. Just with the goal of understanding it. What does it believe? What is it afraid would happen if it stopped doing its job? What does it need from me to feel safe enough to soften, even a little?

I ask these questions with genuine curiosity, not with an agenda. Because the part can smell an agenda from a mile away. If I'm trying to get rid of it, it will dig in harder. If I'm genuinely interested in it, it might start to trust me.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. Walker's framework for understanding the inner critic as a part that developed to manage an unsafe environment applies directly to adult-formed parts as well. The critic isn't always a child part. Sometimes it's the adult version of you that learned to survive by being harder on yourself than anyone else could be.

Stay with me here, because this is the part that matters most. These adult-formed parts don't need to be eliminated. They need to be updated. They need to be told that the emergency is over. They need to be given new information about who you are now, what resources you have, what relationships you're in, what safety is available.

They need to be integrated, not removed. They carry wisdom from real experiences. That wisdom is valuable. It just needs to be held more loosely. It needs to become one perspective among many, not the only perspective running the show.

I think of it like this. Imagine you have a security guard who was hired after a break-in at your home. That guard has been standing at your door for ten years, never sleeping, never resting, checking every person who approaches. The guard is exhausted. The guard is doing a job that was necessary once but might not be necessary anymore. But the guard doesn't know that. The guard still believes the break-in happened yesterday.

Your job isn't to fire the guard. Your job is to walk up to the guard, look them in the eye, and say "I know you've been working so hard. Thank you. The break-in was ten years ago. I have better locks now. I have a security system. I have neighbors who watch out for me. You can rest. You can trust me to handle this. And if something happens, I'll call you back. I promise."

That's the work. That's what it means to work with parts that emerged in adulthood. It's not about dismissing them. It's about earning their trust, showing them the present moment, and inviting them to take a well-deserved rest.

Some parts won't rest immediately. Some parts need to test you first. Some parts need to see you handle a few situations before they believe you're capable. That's okay. That's normal. That's part of the process.

The goal isn't to get rid of these parts. The goal is to have a different relationship with them. One where you're leading, not them. One where they're consulted but not in charge. One where their wisdom is available to you without their fear running your life.

That's possible. I've seen it happen. I've experienced it myself. And it starts with one small shift: recognizing that the adult part of you that seems so solid, so permanent, so "just who you are" might actually be a part. A part that formed to protect you. A part that can learn to trust you. A part that can eventually rest.

Sit with that. See if there's a part of you that just felt something shift when you read those words. That might be one of them. That might be the part that's been waiting for you to see it. Not as a problem to solve. But as a protector to thank.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
